title_en: Al-Mahalli's Original Work: The Makkan Surahs and Al-Fatiha
title_ar: عمل الجلال المحلي الأصلي: السور المكية والفاتحة
content_en:
Al-Mahalli's portion of the tafsir covers Surahs al-Kahf through al-Nas (chapters 18–114), encompassing the shorter Makkan surahs that form the core of the Quran recited in daily prayer, as well as the longer Makkan surahs of the middle Quran. He also wrote the commentary on Surah al-Fatiha, which stands at the opening of the Quran and serves as the gateway to the text as a whole.

The Makkan surahs are characterized by their focus on theological fundamentals: the oneness of God, the reality of the resurrection and judgment, the stories of the prophets and the communities that rejected them, and the nature of the Quran itself. Al-Mahalli's grammatical and lexical method is ideally suited to these surahs, which employ the full range of Arabic rhetorical power — ellipsis, emphasis, contrasting parallel structures, dramatic address — to convey theological truth with aesthetic force.

Surah al-Kahf, which begins al-Mahalli's section, contains four famous narratives: the People of the Cave, the two garden owners, Musa and Khidr, and Dhul-Qarnayn. Each narrative carries theological weight — the first addresses the test of faith and community, the second the test of wealth and ingratitude, the third the limits of human knowledge before divine wisdom, and the fourth the question of righteous kingship and the protection of civilization. Al-Mahalli's commentary clarifies the grammatical structure of each narrative, supplies the elliptical elements, and provides basic contextual glosses without turning the exegesis into a philosophical essay.

The shorter surahs of the final juz — the thirtieth part of the Quran — receive particularly attentive treatment. These surahs, which most Muslims memorize first, are also among the most grammatically dense and rhetorically concentrated in the entire text. The commentary on Surah al-Zalzalah, al-Adiyat, al-Qari'ah, and the other eschatological surahs clarifies the imagery of the Day of Judgment — the shaking of the earth, the weighing of deeds, the scattered people — in straightforward, accessible terms.

Al-Fatiha, which al-Mahalli wrote last (though it appears first in the Quran), receives a commentary that is proportionally longer than most of the short surahs — reflecting its theological density. The seven verses of al-Fatiha contain an affirmation of God's lordship, mercy, and sovereignty over the Day of Judgment, followed by the declaration of worship and petition for guidance, then the request to be placed on the path of those whom God has blessed rather than those who have earned anger or gone astray. Al-Mahalli's commentary identifies the grammatical subject of 'alladhina an'amta alayhim' (those upon whom You have bestowed favor), clarifies the distinction between 'al-maghdub alayhim' and 'al-dallin' as understood in the prophetic tradition, and establishes the verse's context within the surah's overall prayer structure.
